answer:
In "Aashiq Harjaai" ("The Roving Lover"), the faithless (harjaai) lover offers a vivid, paradoxical self-portrait, portraying himself as a restless, mercurial soul embodying irreconcilable contradictions: socially vibrant yet profoundly solitary, spiritually elevated yet earthly, devout yet irreverent, wise yet tinged with madness, dynamic yet paradoxically static, drawn to beauty yet emotionally aloof, and defined by constant change with no loyalty to any single beloved.[^1]

He declares:  
> What a strange collection of contradictions you are, Iqbal!  
> You are the life of the gathering, and yet you are also alone.[^2]  

> Among the beautiful, you are known as one who knows no loyalty;  
> O mercurial one, you are famous and infamous alike.[^3]  

> You have brought into this world the nature of quicksilver;  
> A marvel, this restlessness of yours; how strangely restless you are![^4]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, ch. 28 ("Aashiq Harjaai"), [translation](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/28-aashiq-harjaai/translation/).
[^2]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, ch. 28 ("Aashiq Harjaai"), [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/28-aashiq-harjaai/translation/#p-b540d5).
[^3]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, ch. 28 ("Aashiq Harjaai"), [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/28-aashiq-harjaai/translation/#p-b6eefc).
[^4]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, ch. 28 ("Aashiq Harjaai"), [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/28-aashiq-harjaai/translation/#p-bc0cec).
